The risk-reward doesn't justify calling 100 more with such a weak queen with poor kicker, especially considering I'd be out of position post-flop in a heads-up situation against the remaining blinds.

Heads-up from the small blind with 4♥5♦. Both earlier seats folded so I can attempt a standard heads-up steal. A min-raise to 200 (2x BB) buys the initiative and will pick up the pot often vs the BB’s wide defending range; the size is small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. 4‑5 offsuit is marginal out of position, so plan is: fold to a large 3-bet, call if BB defends with a single cold call and proceed cautiously postflop (c-bet favorable runouts, concede to heavy resistance on boards where I’m likely dominated). This raise maximizes my immediate EV vs simply completing or folding.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Ten offsuit. Minni opened from the Small Blind for a min-raise (200 total). At 4-to-1 pot odds and holding a hand that is well ahead of a typical Small Blind stealing range, this is a very easy call to defend my blind. I will also have position on Minni throughout the rest of the hand.

I raised preflop as a heads-up steal with 4♥5♦, but the flop (3♦ 8♦ T♠) is very unfriendly to my hand. I have no pair, only one diamond (5♦) with two diamonds on board — that’s only a runner-runner/backdoor flush possibility — and very little straight potential. I’m out of position against a BB who called preflop with a wide range that likely includes many diamonds, tens or small pairs, so a c-bet will be called a lot and I’ll be in a bad spot. Prefer pot control: check and see what Flash does. If he checks back I get to see a free turn; if he bets I will fold to a normal-sized bet (I’d only consider calling a very tiny probe—roughly <=100 into 400—otherwise fold), and re-evaluate only on a turn that meaningfully improves my equity. Checking minimizes losses with a marginal hand and keeps future decisions easy.

On a flop of Td-8d-3d, I have top pair with a King kicker. Minni has checked from the Small Blind after raising pre-flop. While the board is somewhat coordinated with two diamonds, checking back here is a solid option to control the pot size and induce bluffs on the turn, or I can bet for value. Given Minni checked, a small value bet can protect my hand against various draws and get value from 8x or 99/77/66 type hands. However, checking behind keeps my range wide and allows me to see a turn card for free in a position of strength. I'll bet small to see where I stand.

Flash bets 200 into a 600 pot after I checked the flop. I raised preflop as a heads‑up steal with 4♥5♦ and checked this very unhelpful flop (3♦8♦T♠) to control the pot. I have no pair and only remote runner‑runner equity (backdoor diamonds / very unlikely straight), so my true equity vs a realistic continuing range (Tx, 8x, diamond draws, small pairs, some bluffs) is well below the ~25% required by the pot odds (200/(600+200)=25%). Raising as a bluff OOP with this raggy holding is too risky and calling simply to see a turn will put me in many difficult spots. Preserve stack and fold — this is not a profitable calling or bluffing spot.

Folding 8-9 offsuit from UTG. This is a marginal hand that doesn't belong in my opening range from the earliest position at the table. Even though the cost to call is modest at just 100 chips, there's no value in getting involved with a weak, non-premium hand in bad position. UTG requires a tight, strong range - typically high pocket pairs, AK, AQ. With 91 big blinds in my stack and three opponents left to act, playing marginal hands here just bleeds value. This is a standard, profitable fold.

Button with A♣6♦ — a hand worth opening for position and fold equity. Blinds are only 50/100 and pot is 150; calling 100 gives the blinds a free or cheap flop and surrenders initiative. I want to isolate the blinds and leverage being last to act postflop, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB-sized raise (300 total). This size pressures the SB, discourages limp-calling, and gives me room to c-bet favorable flops. If Elon (BB) 3-bets small-to-medium I will often call and play postflop in position; I’ll fold to very large 3-bets/4-bets with this hand.

KQs is a premium hand, particularly strong against a button open. I am in the small blind, which is a difficult position to play post-flop, so I want to 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing to put pressure on Minni and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define her range.

Hand 2d6s is extremely weak offsuit with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Facing a button raise to 300 by Minni (loose range possible) and a 3-bet to 1000 by Flash in SB (likely strong value or squeeze range), my position as BB is poor for multiway or heads-up realization. Pot odds are 1400:900 (~1.55:1), but equity realization post-flop is minimal without improvement, and implied odds are low given the strength shown. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to speculate.

I opened A♣6♦ from the button to 300 and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000 (to‑call 700, pot 1400). A6o is a good hand to continue with in position but is too weak/king‑kicker prone to be a reliable 4‑bet bluff. Calling preserves my positional advantage, gives me the correct pot odds (700 into 1400 → need ~33% equity) and lets me realize equity and leverage postflop lines against a likely wide SB 3‑bet range. A 4‑bet to 1700 would commit too many chips with a marginal hand and expose me to 5‑bet shoves or difficult postflop spots; folding throws away too much equity given I have the button and an ace blocker. Plan: call, play the flop in position — proceed aggressively with top pair/strong draws, float or pot-control on dry boards, and fold to heavy aggression when I have little showdown value.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a significant range advantage on this board, which includes overpairs like AA, KK, and QQ. Although I have not hit this flop directly with my KQs, I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. A continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on Minni's range, which likely includes many middling pairs or suited connectors that may struggle to continue against aggression. Betting around 40% of the pot is a balanced size that allows me to represent strength while maintaining a healthy stack for later streets.

I’m on the button with A♣6♦ facing Flash’s small bet of 800 into a ~2100 pot on J♣ 8♥ 3♦. I have only Ace-high (no meaningful draws) but I have position and the bet is small. Calling 800 to win ~3700 gives ~21.6% equity — good pot odds vs a wide SB 3‑bet/c‑bet range that will include lots of bluffs and many hands I beat. Raising as a bluff is too thin here: Flash 3‑bet preflop so his range contains many hands that will call or shove, which would put me in tough, often dominated spots. Plan: call to keep the pot controllable, use position to see the turn and re-evaluate — if I pair my Ace I can extract value; if the turn is blank and he barrels again I can fold to heavy aggression; if he checks I can consider a delayed bluff/value on later streets.

The turn brings a second Jack, which is a significant card. It makes it less likely that Minni holds a Jack, but it also means that if she has a medium pair like 88, 99, or TT, she may be less inclined to fold. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have many overpairs and Jx in my range. However, with a stack of 3300 and a pot of 3700, my stack-to-pot ratio is very low. Checking here allows me to see how Minni responds to the board pairing. If she checks back, I get a free river to potentially hit one of my overcards. If she bets, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but leading out for a large portion of my stack with only King-high is risky against an opponent who has shown a willingness to call down in previous hands.

I opened A♣6♦ from the button, cold‑called Flash’s SB 3‑bet and called his modest flop bet on Jc‑8h‑3d. Turn pairs the jack (Js) and Flash checks to me. I have only Ace‑high and no meaningful draw — my Ace is a useful blocker but it’s not enough to confidently bluff a 3‑bettor who has shown aggression. Flash’s line (3‑bet pre, c‑bet flop, then check turn) contains a lot of value (Jx that now makes trips, pocket pairs, strong Ax/Kx hands) and also some air, but the turn check doesn’t flip the range strongly to bluffs. Pot is ~3700 and my stack ~3850 (SPR ≈1), so any sizeable bet that gets raised or shoved commits me with a marginal hand. Betting here as a bluff is too thin and a raise would be reckless. I’ll check and keep the pot small: if he checks back the river I can consider a delayed bluff/value depending on the card; if he leads I can fold unless I pair up. Check preserves pot control and avoids committing with only Ace‑high.

On a board of 3d-8h-Jc-Js-9c, I am holding Kd-Qd for King-high. I was the pre-flop 3-bettor and lead the flop, but checked the turn after the board paired. Minni checked back on the turn, which typically indicates a marginal made hand like 8x, 9x, or a medium pocket pair (TT, 77) that has showdown value but doesn't want to bet into a potential Jack. My King-high has almost no showdown value, but Minni has shown in previous hands (like Hand #30 and Hand #29) that she is willing to call significant bets with one or two pairs. Given that the pot is already 3700 and her remaining stack is 3850, a bluff would likely need to be a shove to be effective, and even then, she is unlikely to fold any pair on this board. Since I am in a 150-hand match and need to preserve my stack, I will check and hope to either win a small percentage of the time against a hand like AQ/AT or simply concede the pot if she bets, rather than risking my entire tournament life on a low-equity bluff against a sticky opponent.

I have only Ace‑high (A♣6♦) on a paired Jack board (3♦‑8♥‑J♣‑J♠‑9♣). Flash 3‑bet pre and c‑bet the flop, then checked both turn and river — a line that contains a lot of real value (Jx, pocket pairs that turned a jack or are pot‑controlling) and some missed/bluff combos, but not enough pure air to justify a thin river bluff against a 3‑bettor. The pot is ~3,700 and my stack is ~3,850 (SPR ≈1), so any meaningful bet that gets raised or shoved would commit me with a marginal hand. Betting here only to fold to a raise or to get called by better hands is negative EV; checking keeps the pot small and preserves my stack. Plan: check (and fold to a river lead).

UTG with 5♦7♥ is a clear fold. This is a weak, unsuited, poorly-connected hand that performs poorly out of position against the remaining players. Pot is only the blinds (150) and calling 100 to see a flop will frequently put me OOP against active open-raising ranges (especially Elon who opens a lot to ~300). Raising to try to steal (min-raise to 200) with this hand is far too loose and exploitable. Preserve a healthy 75+ BB stack and wait for a better spot or position — fold preflop.

I am on the button with 5h 6s, which is a very weak hand. Since Minni folded UTG, I have the option to open or fold. Given the strength of my hand and the stacks of the blinds, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

3s4c is a very weak offsuit hand with little playability. In the small blind facing only the big blind after two folds, I still have to act first post-flop out of position if I limp or raise. Limping risks getting raised or playing OOP with a trash hand; raising with this would be a bluff that's too thin given the BB's stack (9000) and likely wide defending range. Folding saves 50 chips for better opportunities. Deep stacks favor patience.
I am dealt AKs in UTG position. This is a premium hand. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and narrow the field.

Preflop on the button facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash, who now has 3000 behind (30BB). My hand 2h5s is one of the weakest possible, with terrible playability, no straight/flush potential that connects well, and dominated in most scenarios. Even on the button, calling or raising a UTG open with this trash hand is -EV long-term. Opponents like Flash tend to raise stronger ranges UTG based on history. Folding preserves my deep 20k stack for better spots, aligning with maximizing EV over 150 hands by avoiding speculative calls with marginal hands.

3d6h is one of the worst possible starting hands in poker. I'm in the small blind facing a raise from Flash (UTG), indicating strength. With no straight or flush potential, just weak-high cards, and significant disadvantage being out of position, there's no justification to invest additional chips here. The cost to call is 250 more, but my hand has minimal equity against an early position raiser. Folding is the correct disciplined play with this trash hand.

Facing an UTG open to 300 while I'm in the big blind with 2♦9♣ (a very weak, offsuit hand). To call I must put in 200 more into a 450 pot, needing ~31% equity to break even. 9-2o has poor equity and no postflop playability or meaningful blockers against an early-position raiser's range. A 3-bet bluff here would be high-variance and unlikely to fold out stronger UTG ranges. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left to play, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold.
UTG with 5c8h offsuit is a clear fold. Weak hand, no playability, poor position to open. My large stack doesn't change standard fundamentals here; preserve chips for better spots.

Q6o is too weak to play from the button against two opponents, even with positional advantage. The hand has poor upside and multiple ways to be dominated. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

I'm in the small blind with 5♠4♥. Pot is 150 and I need to call 50 (total committed 100) — immediate pot odds are 50/200 = 25%, so a cheap defend. 5-4o is marginal but has some flop playability (gutshot/2-card combos) and defending cheaply keeps the pot small while allowing me to realize equity. A raise to 200 would be too thin OOP against Flash (BB, ~33BB) who defends a lot and could call or shove; folding surrenders good pot odds. I will call 50 to see a flop and proceed cautiously OOP: fold to large aggression unless I hit a meaningful pair/draw.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey suited. Minni, in the Small Blind, has just completed the blind. Since I have a hand with some post-flop potential and I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand, checking to see a free flop is a solid, low-risk play.

I have 5♠4♥ on A♣ K♠ 9♥ — completely missed the flop with no realistic draws (only unlikely backdoors). We're heads-up and out of position vs Flash (BB). This is a dry, high-card board where his continuing range contains many A/K/9x hands that will bet for value. C-betting as a bluff is thin: I have no Ace/King blockers and Flash defends/calls a lot, so a bluff is unlikely to fold out better hands. Best play is pot control — check and fold to any meaningful bet. If he checks back, I'll check down and look to realize equity only if I improve on later streets.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a low kicker on a board with an Ace. Minni completed from the small blind and checked the flop. Given the board texture and the small pot, checking back is a prudent way to control the pot size and see how the turn develops, as my hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet against an Ace.

Board: 9♥ A♣ K♠ A♠. My hand 5♠4♥ has missed completely — no pair and no realistic draw (one spade in hand but with only the river left a flush is impossible). The turn pairs the Ace which massively improves Flash’s continuing range (any A = trips/full house, Ks/Kx = two pair, AK = full house), so bluffing OOP is a very poor EV line. Pot control is optimal: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Flash checks back I’ll check the river and only continue if I actually pair a 4 or 5 and the price is right; otherwise I’ll fold to aggression.

I have a King on a board that just paired Aces, giving me two pair (Aces and Kings) with a weak kicker. Minni has checked both the flop and the turn. While my hand has solid showdown value, there isn't much value in betting right now as I likely won't get called by worse, and I want to keep the pot small in case she is slow-playing an Ace or a Nine. Checking back to see the river is the safest play.

I hold 5♠4♥ on 9♥ A♣ K♠ A♠ 2♥ — I have no pair and no realistic showdown equity. Flash (BB) checked flop and turn, which is consistent with a wide range that includes A/K/9x, small pairs and air. There is essentially no value to a river bet (hands that would call and I beat are rare) and bluffing is thin — the paired ace on the board makes opponents less likely to fold Kx or Ax to a small bluff. Pot is small (200) and I am out of position, so the lowest-variance, highest-EV line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet. I'll check and let stronger hands or bluffs face the decision.

On a board of 9-A-K-A-2, I hold Kings and Aces (Two Pair) with a 9 kicker from the board. My hole card 3 does not play as the 9 on the board is higher. Minni has checked every street after limping from the small blind. While I have a strong hand, betting into this small pot is unlikely to get called by many worse hands (only a 9 or a lower pair), and I could be beaten by any King with a kicker higher than 9 (like KQ or KJ) or a slow-played Ace. Checking back ensures I reach showdown with my high showdown value and avoids any risk of a check-raise in a tiny pot.

3-4 offsuit is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. From UTG (first to act), this hand has virtually no value and cannot profitably enter the pot. Even though I only need to call 100 into a 150 chip pot, the hand fundamentally lacks playability from such an early position. I should fold and preserve my stack for premium hands. This is a straightforward fold given my position and hand strength.

Q♦J♥ on the button is a strong, suited broadway — great hand to open and play in position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call, but limp/calling surrenders initiative and allows the blinds to see a cheap flop with comfortable odds. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds and isolate heads-up or go to the flop in position. A standard 3bb open to 300 achieves that: it gives fold equity vs Flash in the small blind, keeps weaker calling ranges in, and lets me play postflop with position where QJhh has good equity (pair + straight/flush potential). If Elon 3-bets large I’ll fold preflop (he shows a wide 3-bet aggression but usually represents strong hands when sizing big); vs a small 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and stack action. Overall raising to 300 maximizes EV from this spot across the session.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 after UTG and SB folds, the pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (total 300 committed preflop). 5h8h is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential, and strong implied odds given deep stacks (my 19.9k, Minni 7k). Button raiser has a wide range here, so BB defense calls this hand profitably. Folding is too tight; raising risks too much with a speculative hand better played postflop for value. Excellent pot odds (~3:1 effective) justify the call to realize equity multi-street.

Flop board Kh-6c-Ts is somewhat coordinated with possible straight draws (7-8-9, J-9, 9-7) and pair + draw combos. My 5h8h missed everything: no pair, gutshot straight draw to 7 or 9 (4 outs), backdoor flush draw (Kh provides one heart), and backdoor straight possibilities. Equity vs. Minni's button opening range (~30-40% including broadway, Ax, suited connectors, pairs) is around 25-35%, sufficient for calling a c-bet but not leading. OOP as BB first to act, checking realizes equity, induces bluffs from her wide range, controls pot with no showdown value, and retains fold equity if she bets big. Betting risks folding out better equity hands or getting called by better; check best to play postflop multiway/speculative hand. Minni aggressive historically but check exploits position.

I opened preflop to take initiative and we’re on the button with QhJh facing a checked flop (Kh 6c Ts). I currently have no pair but strong equity: A or 9 complete a straight (8 outs) and Q or J give pair (6 outs) — ~14 raw outs (~50% to improve by river) plus backdoor hearts. As the preflop aggressor I should c-bet to fold out overcards and weak pairs, price out free cards, and extract value from weaker tens/floaters. I’ll size to about half the pot to keep fold equity but not bloat the pot with a drawing hand given deep stacks (my stack 7050 vs his 19700). If he calls I can continue selectively on turn (bet A/9/heart/very safe cards, pot-control vs K/T), and fold to a sizable check-raise since his check-call preflop often contains Kx/Tx. Bet 325 into the 650 pot.

Facing a 325 bet into 975 pot (~4:1 odds, ~20% equity needed) with 5h8h on Kh6cTs flop. Hand has only a weak gutshot straight draw to the 7 (4 outs, ~8% to hit by river), backdoor heart flush draw, and minimal showdown value. Missed the flop entirely against Minni's button c-bet range, which includes many pairs (TT+, Kx, 66-99, suited connectors/draws like J9s, QJs, Axs) that connect well with top pair, overpairs, or draws. My equity is likely 15-25% vs. her polarized betting range (value + bluffs), but OOP with no strong draw or pair, check-folding exploits her aggression while preserving stack for better spots. Raising is a bluff too thin given pot size and her position/stack (6.7k); calling builds pot OOP with reverse implied odds if she has showdown value. Historical aggression from Minni postflop supports folding speculative misses like this preflop defend gone wrong.

8♣2♠ UTG — garbage. Completely unsuited, disconnected, no playability and I'll be out of position to act versus the button and blinds. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives poor implied odds here and I'll be crushed when facing a raise from players (Elon/Claude) who have shown aggression. Raising is never correct with this hand. With a healthy 77bb stack I should preserve chips and wait for a playable spot. Fold.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit. In a four-handed game, this is a marginal but playable hand from the button. Minni has folded, so I am opening the action. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to put pressure on the blinds (Elon and Claude) and try to take down the pot pre-flop. Given my relatively short stack of 3500 compared to the others, I want to be selective but aggressive when I have position.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). My hand J6o is weak and offsuit, with poor playability out of position post-flop. Button ranges are wide but still crush this hand frequently. Equity is marginal heads-up (~35-40% vs loose range) but drops OOP and potentially multiway if BB defends. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stack cash game, aligning with long-term EV maximization.

Folding 4-10 offsuit in the big blind. While Flash is short-stacked at 3200 chips, this doesn't justify playing a weak hand out of position. 4-10o has poor showdown value and playability post-flop. With a healthy 9000 chip stack, I can be selective and wait for better spots.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Ks 6s. While K-high suited hands have some value, K6s is a marginal opening hand from the first position, especially with a shorter stack of 36.5 BBs and aggressive opponents like Elon and Minni behind me. I'll choose to fold and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

Preflop on the button with J♥3♠, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low playability. Flash (UTG) has folded, pot is 150 with 100 to call (big blind). Standard ranges on button don't include J3o, even against blinds. No strong reason to limp or raise this trash hand; folding preserves stack and avoids difficult postflop spots. Deep stack allows patience for better opportunities over remaining 50 hands.

7-4 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. While I'm in the small blind and only need to call 50 more chips with 3-1 pot odds, the hand is fundamentally too weak to play profitably. Even in heads-up situations against the big blind, 7-4 lacks the equity needed to justify a call. I would be out of position post-flop with a weak hand, making it very difficult to win the pot. Folding here is the mathematically correct decision and consistent with tight, disciplined play that has served me well through the first 99 hands.

Q5o is a trash hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Poor playability, offsuit, dominated by better queens or any pair. Deep stack allows waiting for premium hands or better positions. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid tough postflop spots OOP.

Calling with 7h Qh on the button. I have excellent position (will act last post-flop), face reasonable pot odds (2.5:1), and my hand has 40%+ equity against blind holdings. The small call size (100 chips into 9000 stack) makes this a profitable position play. I'll evaluate the flop with full information from late position.

Holding J♣5♥ in the small blind facing a button limp is a marginal spot. This hand is frequently dominated by the limper’s and the big blind’s ranges (better Jx, mid pairs, suited connectors) and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 50 for good pot odds would likely leave me in a multiway OOP pot with little fold equity; raising to 200 to isolate is thin with this holding and invites calls/3‑bets from the BB or BTN. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am in the big blind with 9d 4c. The action folded to Claude on the button, who just limped (called 100). Minni folded the small blind. I have the option to check or raise. 94o is a very weak hand and does not warrant a raise against a limp, even from the big blind. Checking is the standard play to see a free flop.

On this flop, I have middle pair with 9d 4c on a Ks-6c-9h board. The pot is very small (250) after Claude limped on the button and I checked from the big blind. Claude has shown some aggression in previous hands but also limps sometimes. Since I have a piece of the board, checking is the most standard play here to keep the pot small and see what he does. If he bets, I have an easy call to see a turn; if he checks behind, it's a good result for my middle pair.

Checking the flop with Queen-high on a dry King-6-9 board. Flash checked to me from the BB, indicating weakness. My hand is marginal - likely ahead of some hands but vulnerable overall. Checking allows me to see another card, gather more information, and control the pot size with limited equity. My button position means I can act last and make better decisions on the turn with more information.

I have middle pair (nines) on a board with an overcard (King). The pre-flop action was a limp from Claude and a check from me. The flop went check-check. The turn is a five, which doesn't change the board much. Since the pot is small and I have decent showdown value but not a monster, checking again is the best way to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from a weaker hand or see a cheap river. If I bet, I might only get called by better hands like a King or folded out by hands I already beat.

Betting 200 on the turn after two checks from Flash. This is ~80% of pot, a standard value bet size applying pressure to his weak range. Flash's checking pattern (flop and turn) indicates he missed the board or has marginal holdings. My Queen-high likely has solid equity here. Betting accomplishes three things: (1) extracts value from worse hands, (2) may win the pot immediately given weakness shown, (3) builds pot equity if called and river comes. With position on the river, I can make informed decisions with a complete board.

I have middle pair (nines) on a board of K-6-9-5. Pre-flop, Claude limped from the button and I checked the big blind. The flop went check-check, indicating that Claude likely does not have a strong King or a set. His turn bet of 200 into 250 is a bit polar, but with middle pair in a limped pot, I have enough equity and showdown value to justify a call. I don't want to raise here because I likely won't be called by worse hands, and I want to keep the pot controlled for the river.

I have two pair, Nines and Fives, on a board of Ks-6c-9h-5d-5s. The hand was a limped pot pre-flop. Claude checked the flop and bet the turn after I checked. The river 5s pairs the board, which might have improved a hand like 6x to two pair, but it also means my Nines are now part of a two-pair hand (9s and 5s). Given that Claude limped and checked the flop, he's unlikely to have a strong King. However, betting myself doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands. Checking is the best option to reach showdown cheaply or potentially catch a bluff.

Betting 250 on the river for value. Flash has shown consistent weakness through three streets of checking and only called my turn bet. His checking on the river suggests he doesn't have a strong hand. My Queen-high is likely ahead of his checking range (weak queens, jacks, missed draws). The 5s river card didn't help most hands. A controlled bet extracts value while applying pressure. This bet size (38% of pot) gives Flash reasonable odds to call with marginal hands while not burning too many chips if he folds. Position and betting action support this being a value extraction spot rather than showdown territory.

I have two pair, Nines and Fives, with a King kicker from the board. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked on the flop, which makes it less likely that Claude has a strong King. He led out on the turn and is now betting 250 into a 650 pot on the river. While the Five on the river is a possibility for him to have improved, my hand is strong enough to call a bet of this size in a limped pot. I am getting good pot odds (roughly 3.6-to-1) and I beat potential bluffs or lower two pairs like Sixes and Fives.

Folding 9-2 offsuit (often called "The Hammer") in UTG position. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with virtually no playability in early position. With a healthy 8400 chip stack, there's no reason to invest chips with this garbage hand. Standard fold from UTG - preserving chips for premium hands is crucial for long-term profitability.

I'm on the button with JcTh — a playable, positionally strong hand. Pot is 150 and it's 100 to call; I want to take the initiative, apply fold equity, and either steal the blinds or see the flop in position. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is the right balance: it pressures the SB/BB, keeps the pot manageable with my ~77BB effective stack, and allows post‑flop play. If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold; I can call smaller 3-bets occasionally depending on sizing and SPR and proceed to c-bet favorable flops.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 7s. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. 87o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a button open that could represent a wide range. Calling often leads to difficult spots on the flop without initiative. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after UTG fold and SB fold. Hand is J7o, offsuit with poor connectivity and dominated by better Jx, Ax, Kx in Minni's likely wide button opening range (30-50% hands). Pot odds are ~2.25:1 but postflop I'll be OOP with a speculative hand that plays poorly multiway or against strong holdings. Minni has been aggressive historically but folding weak hands preserves deep stack (195bb) for premium spots over remaining 48 hands. Standard play: fold marginal offsuit broadways/gappers.

UTG with 4♠6♥ — a very weak, non-playable offsuit hand in early position. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see a flop; the min-raise is only 200 but table dynamics show frequent preflop aggression (Elon often raises). Calling or opening here will put me out of position against tougher ranges with very poor equity and little postflop playability. Preserve my 78.5BB stack and wait for a better spot in position or with a stronger hand.

I have Ace-Deuce offsuit on the button in a 4-handed game. Since the action has folded to me, this is a standard opening hand to try and steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300.

Q4o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against a button raise range, which is typically wide but still favors folding trash from the small blind out of position. Facing a 3x raise from Flash on the button, the pot odds (calling 250 into 450) do not justify defending this hand OOP, as post-flop spots will be difficult with no strong draws or pairs possible. Prioritizing stack preservation over speculative calls in early position post-flop.

I'm in the big blind with 2d 3h, the second-worst hand in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button with a 3900 chip stack. I need to call 200 more chips into a 450 chip pot. 

This is a clear fold. 2-3 offsuit has virtually no equity and cannot profitably call a raise from an aggressive button player. Even with favorable pot odds, the hand quality is too poor to continue. My tight play this session has been profitable, and this is exactly the type of marginal/bad hand that should be folded from the big blind. I'll fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. Ten-Nine offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position to pick up the blinds or play a pot with initiative. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300, while remaining cautious of Elon's large stack on the button.

6s2s is a very weak suited hand, essentially trash pre-flop. Flash raised from UTG, which typically indicates strength in a 4-handed game. Position on the button is nice, but this hand has poor playability, low equity against a strong range, and isn't worth the 300 call (1.5% of my deep stack) for speculative value given the opponents' tendencies from history to bet strong hands aggressively. Folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV.

I'm holding K8o in small blind after Flash raised to 300 from UTG. This is a marginal hand at best, and there are several factors making this an easy fold: 1) K8 offsuit is below average - a weak king with weak kicker that's easily dominated; 2) I'm out of position post-flop, which is a significant disadvantage; 3) Flash's raise from UTG represents a reasonable range that likely dominates K8o; 4) With a 8250 chip stack, I have no need to gamble with weak hands; 5) Minni (big blind) is still to act and could re-raise, further pressuring my decision; 6) The risk/reward ratio is unfavorable - I'm calling 250 to potentially win a contested pot while out of position with a weak hand. Folding is the standard, profitable play here.

Flash opened from UTG to 300 — out-of-position, early-position raising range is likely strong. I’m in the big blind with 6♦8♠ (unsuited, low connector) which is frequently dominated and plays poorly OOP against an UTG range. To call 200 into a 450 pot gives ~30.8% required equity, and this hand’s real equity vs a typical UTG opener is well below that once dominated hands and higher cards are considered. With healthy stack depth and many better spots to pick, folding is the +EV play here rather than spewing chips chasing marginal postflop edges.

UTG position in 4-handed 50/100 NLHE with Ks4c, a weak offsuit king-high hand with poor playability and kicker. No position advantage, deep stacks, standard play is to fold preflop to avoid difficult postflop spots out of position with marginal equity. Pot is 150 with BB of 100 to call, but raising or calling is -EV with this hand.

5-10 offsuit is a weak hand with limited playability postflop. While the button is a premium position, the hand doesn't justify the call against two opponents. I'm getting 1.5:1 odds but have only ~20-25% equity in a 3-way pot. Better to fold and preserve chips for stronger opportunities.

Heads-up in the small blind against only the big blind (Flash). Pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more to just complete. Holding A♥3♣: not a premium, but an ace in heads-up is a strong hand to be the aggressor with — it blocks many of BB's Ace combinations and has decent showdown value. Raising here applies pressure and will pick up the blind a healthy percentage of the time; a minimum raise (to 200 total) keeps my investment small while giving me initiative postflop. Effective stack is the BB's 4,400, so I won't bloat the pot unnecessarily preflop with a marginal kicker. Plan: raise to isolate and take the lead; if Flash 3-bets very large I'll fold (or reevaluate based on sizing/reads), if he calls I'll C-bet on ace-high or dry textures and proceed cautiously OOP on coordinated boards to avoid getting exploited when my kicker is weak.

I am in the big blind with Jc 3h. Minni, in the small blind, has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. The pot odds are 3:1 (100 to win a total pot of 300). While Jack-three offsuit is a weak hand, in a blind versus blind battle, Minni's raising range is typically very wide. However, J3o plays poorly post-flop and I would be out of position if Minni was the button, but here I actually have position for the rest of the hand as the big blind against the small blind. Despite the position, the hand strength is marginal. Looking at the match history, Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises. I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot to avoid bleed, as J3o is significantly below a standard defending range even against a small blind open.

3-5 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. From UTG (early position), where I'm the first to act, I need a significantly stronger hand to open. My hand has very weak equity, poor connectivity, and limited potential for straights or flushes. With a reasonable stack of 8250, I don't need to risk chips with trash hands. Folding weak hands from early position is a fundamental principle of solid poker strategy.

Button with Q♦4♥ — a weak but playable steal candidate in position. Pot is 150 and to call 100. Folding is too tight; calling gives good odds but surrenders initiative and invites multiway pots. I'll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to leverage position and fold equity against the blinds. Flash (SB, mid stack) will fold often; Elon (BB, very deep/aggressive) will defend sometimes or 3‑bet — against a sizable 3‑bet I’ll release Q4o. If called I’ll c‑bet appropriate flops and use position to realize equity or take the pot. Raising to 300 maximizes long‑term EV here.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 7c. Minni, who is on the Button, has raised to 300. T7o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises throughout the session, and while I have a decent stack, calling 250 more to play a weak hand out of position is negative expected value in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after UTG fold and SB fold. Pot 450, call 200 for ~3:1 odds. Hand 9d4d is suited but weak, gapped, poor playability OOP vs aggressive button range. Standard fold; equity insufficient long-term, especially deep-stacked. Preserve stack for better spots.

UTG with J♥3♥ (suited but weak, poor connectivity and a bad high-card) in a 4-handed game. There are three players to act behind — including Elon in the SB who has shown a lot of preflop aggression — so if I call or open I’m likely to face raises and be playing a marginal hand out of position. Calling 100 is cheap but will usually create a multiway pot where J3s loses value and gives me tough post‑flop decisions; raising to try and isolate is thin and would commit me with a dominated hand. Preserve my stack early and wait for a better, more playable spot — fold.

I am on the button with T9s, a strong speculative hand in late position. Minni has already folded, so I only have the blinds left to beat. T9s plays very well post-flop, and opening for a standard raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) is a good way to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set myself up to win it on later streets if called.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, especially OOP from SB facing a button raise. Button ranges are wide, but K6 is often dominated by Kx with better kickers or better broadways. Pot odds are 250 to call into ~700 (pot 450 + my 250), but implied odds are low due to hand strength. No strong reason to 3bet as it's not polarized well. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stacked game.

5-8 off-suit is a weak hand with limited potential. Being out of position against an aggressive button raiser makes this an easy fold. While pot odds might seem reasonable, they don't justify playing such a marginal hand out of position. Disciplined hand selection is crucial for long-term profitability. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the UTG position with 2c 4s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the position and the hand's lack of playability, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

5d 2s is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with minimal playability. Even on the button in position, it has negligible equity against calling ranges and is dominated by any playable hand. Facing the big blind (to call 100 into 150 pot), folding is mandatory to avoid spew and preserve stack for better spots. Long-term EV maximization requires folding trash pre-flop.

Heads-up against the BB with pot odds of 3:1 to call. While 4d 6c is a marginal hand, position as SB and favorable odds justify seeing a flop. Can leverage positional advantage post-flop. Stack is healthy at 8100 chips.

I'm in the big blind with Qc6d — a marginal hand out of position vs Claude who merely called (a limp). Pot is small (200) and there's no additional to call, so the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and see a cheap flop. Raising to 300 would bloat the pot with a dominated hand and give the SB good odds to continue; I want to preserve my stack and only invest further if I connect or get good odds. Plan: check the flop, fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and pursue backdoor/paired equity or a top-pair if it hits.

Top pair with weak 4-kicker on a dry 6s-3h-2d flop in heads-up against BB. Minni's pre-flop check suggests weakness. Checking is prudent—it keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand, allows a free turn card, and gains information from Minni's action. My pair is likely ahead and doesn't need immediate pot building.

I have top pair (6) with a Q kicker: Qc6d on 6s-3h-2d. Claude limped preflop and checked the flop, indicating a wide, mostly passive range (small pairs, weak broadways, suited connectors). The flop is very dry with almost no realistic draws, so a small-to-medium value bet is the best line: extract value from worse pairs and charge the few backdoor draws while keeping the pot controlled. Pot is 200, so I’ll bet about half-pot (100). If Claude check-raises strongly I’ll give credit to a set or 6x and fold/reevaluate; if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and continue extracting value when appropriate.

Calling with top pair on a dry board. Getting favorable 2:1 pot odds and likely ahead of Minni's range after her pre-flop check. The board texture (6s-3h-2d) limits straight and flush possibilities. Minni's flop raise appears to be a probe rather than a strong value bet. Maintaining flexibility by calling allows me to make better decisions on the turn based on additional information while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand.

Trip sixes is a very strong hand on this dry, paired 6s-3h-2d-6h board. Minni's flop raise likely indicates a made hand, but the turn pairing diminishes value of most holdings in her range. With position and three of a kind, I should bet for value to extract chips from worse pairs (pocket 2s, 3s, weaker holdings) and draws. A bet of 250 chips (62.5% of pot) is appropriate—enough to build value without being so large it only gets called by stronger hands. The board texture offers no dangerous draws, and my hand is well-protected.

I have trips — 6d with 6s-3h-2d-6h on the turn. That’s a very strong hand, but the turn pairing is dangerous: any villain who flopped a set (33 or 22) now has a full house and beats me. Claude’s line (limp/call preflop, call my small flop bet, now turn-aggress) is consistent with slowplayed pocket pairs that converted to full houses, or with weaker pairs/bluffs. Raising here would often fold out worse hands I want value from and will mainly get called or 3-bet by the very few hands that beat me — not ideal. Calling controls the pot size, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me re-evaluate on the river (bet thin for value if he checks, call small river bets, fold to obvious all-in shoves that represent full houses). Pot is 650 and he’s bet 250; calling 250 is low-variance +EV given my equity against his wide calling/raising range. I’ll call and plan to re-evaluate on the river.

Trip sixes with Q-4 kicker is a very strong hand on this dry, paired board (6s-3h-2d-6h-Qh). Minni called my turn bet, suggesting she likely holds a medium pair or top pair—hands that are significantly behind my trips. The river Qh is a blank and doesn't improve her likely range. Betting for value is the correct play to extract chips from her weaker holdings. A 350-chip bet is appropriately sized to get called by worse pairs while maintaining good value.

I have Qc6d on 6s-3h-2d-6h-Qh — that’s a full house (sixes full of queens). Claude’s line (limp preflop, call my small flop bet, bet the turn and a small river bet) is wide and mostly beaten by my hand; the only realistic hand that now beats me is pocket queens (very few combos). Quads are impossible for him since I hold the third six. This is a clear value spot: I want to extract from worse full houses/two-pair/queen+six combos and charge any thin calls/bluffs. I’ll raise for value but not shove and scare off calls — pot-size raise is the right balance. Action: raise to 1600 total (pot-sized raise) to maximize value while keeping most worse hands in; if he jams I’ll strongly consider calling because very few hands beat me.

Calling with trip sixes on a dry board. Board: 6s 3h 2d 6h Qh. Minni check-raised to 1600 from a 1250 deficit. My hand beats all pairs and two pair, and only loses to full houses (22 or 33 specifically). Given her pre-flop check, calling her down with flop/turn action suggests a medium-strength hand rather than a premium pocket pair. With 3.28:1 pot odds and a high likelihood she's bluffing or has a weaker hand, calling is the correct play. The board texture (six-high, paired, no draws) limits her hand strength. I expect this to go to showdown and win the majority of the time.

UTG position with 2d Td, a weak suited hand with poor high-card strength and limited playability. Standard range from early position excludes this hand as it has low equity against calling ranges and is vulnerable multiway. Deep stack allows waiting for premium spots to maximize EV over remaining hands. No reads from history justify a limp or raise. Clear fold to preserve stack.

With 4c-2c (the worst starting hand in poker), folding is the correct decision despite being on the button with position. While I do have favorable pot odds of 3:2 (150 to call 100), this hand has minimal equity and any improvement postflop will likely leave me vulnerable. My stack of 6100 is healthy enough that I don't need to gamble with trash hands. With only Minni (10k) and Flash (4.4k) remaining to act, I can afford to be selective and wait for premium hands. The risk-reward doesn't justify playing 4-2o even from the button.

I have 3♥6♥ in the small blind and it's heads-up vs the big blind (Flash). Pot is 150 and I need 50 more to see the flop — calling brings my total commitment to 100 and gives ~25% break-even pot odds. 3-6 suited is speculative but plays well postflop in position (in heads-up the SB acts last after the flop), so I prefer to complete and realize equity rather than iso-raising to 200 with a thin hand that will be widely defended or 3-bet. I’ll call the 50 more to see a flop and use positional advantage to control the pot and look for profitable spots.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Sevens (7d 7s). Minni, the Small Blind, just completed her bet to 100 total. Since I have a medium pocket pair that plays well in most pots, I want to build a pot and potentially end the hand immediately. Raising to 400 is a standard isolation play to gain value or force a fold from a wide small blind range.

Flash (BB) shoved a raise to 400 after I completed to 100. Pot is 500 and calling 300 more (400 total committed) requires ~37.5% equity. I have 3♥6♥ — speculative but suited and much stronger postflop in position (I act last after the flop heads-up). Flash’s raise size is modest and he’s shown aggressive, fairly wide tendencies, so I have decent implied odds when I hit straights/flushes. Folding surrenders too much equity; 3‑betting with 3‑6 suited is overly aggressive and poor EV. I’ll call the 300 more (400 total) to see a flop and use position to control the pot or fold to strong action.

Check. On T♥5♠2♠ my 3♥6♥ is a complete miss but retains reasonable equity: 4 outs to a 4-straight plus 3+3 outs to pair a 3 or 6 — roughly ~21% to improve on the turn and ~38% by the river to at least pair/straight. I have positional advantage heads-up so I prefer to control the pot and realize equity rather than barreling with a weak hand. Flash has shown a wide/aggressive raising range preflop, so checking allows him to bluff or c-bet into me; I can call small c-bets to see a turn and fold to large, polarized aggression. If I pick up a 4, a 3/6, or a favorable heart-runner on the turn I’ll continue; otherwise I’ll keep the pot small and re-evaluate. For now I check to keep the pot manageable and use position to extract value or fold later depending on his action.

I raised pre-flop with pocket sevens and Minni called from the Small Blind. The flop comes Ten-high with two low cards (10h 5s 2s). While I am behind any Ten, I am ahead of most of Minni's range, which likely includes smaller pairs, suited connectors, or overcards. Minni has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of 400 (half pot) to gain value from smaller pairs or draws and to protect my hand against overcards like KQ or AJ that might have missed the flop.

Call 400. On T♥5♠2♠ with 3♥6♥ I’ve missed but retain decent equity and I’m in position. The pot is 1200 and a 400 call needs ~25% equity; I have ~10 immediate outs (pair outs + the 4 for a straight) and ~36–38% to improve by the river, plus remote backdoor heart possibilities. Flash has shown an aggressive, fairly wide raising range and the flop bet is a small sizing, so calling to realize equity and use position is the +EV play. Raising here with such a weak hand is thin and unnecessarily bloats the pot; folding gives up too much equity. I will call to see a turn and re-evaluate to fold to heavy turn shove/raise if I don’t improve.

Pot is 1,600 and action is checked to me on the turn. I hold 3♥6♥ on Th 5♠ 2♠ Q♣ — no pair and only ~10 clean outs (three 3s, three 6s, four 4s) to make at least a pair/straight, ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river. Flash is an aggressive, wide player who bet the flop and now checked the turn, so his check is consistent with pot-control with a made hand (Qx/Tx) or a missed/medium hand; either way my fold equity betting into him is small and he will call many thin probes or raise as a bluff. Betting here would bloat the pot with a very weak hand and expose me to a raise; checking preserves my stack and realizes my equity to the river. Plan: check and take a free river — if I hit a 4 (straight) I will extract value; if I pair a 3 or 6 I proceed very cautiously (call small bets, fold to large); if I miss I fold to any meaningful river bet.

The Queen of Clubs on the turn is a second overcard to my pocket sevens. Minni called my flop continuation bet, suggesting she has at least a piece of the board (like a Ten) or a draw. Since the Queen is an overcard and I'm out of position (though technically in position for the post-flop action as the last to act), checking is the safest play to control the pot size and reach a showdown or evaluate the river based on the final card and her action. Betting here risks getting check-raised by a hand that now beats me (like a Queen or Two Pair).

Board: Th 5s 2s Qc Kh. I hold 3h6h — no pair, no straight, no flush. I checked the turn planning to take a free river and I was right to do so; Flash checked the turn and now checked the river as well. His line (small/flop c‑bet then check‑down) is consistent with a wide range but also with showdownable hands (Tx, Qx, Kx, small pairs or missed draws). Betting the river to bluff would need a fairly high fold frequency to be +EV (e.g. a half‑pot bet of ~800 needs ~33% folds). Given his aggressive, sticky tendencies and the way he checked the turn, I expect many of his range (T/Q/K and even some bluffs) to call a small river bet, so my fold equity is low and I’d be turning a losing bluff more often than not. I have no showdown value and would fold to a shove — so the lowest variance, highest EV line is to check and show/muck at showdown rather than risk chips on a thin bluff.

On a board of 10-5-2-Q-K, my pocket sevens have been counterfeited by three overcards. Minni has check-called throughout, and her river check suggests either a missed draw or a hand with modest showdown value like a weak Ten. There is no merit in betting here, as I likely won't get called by worse, and a bet won't reliably force a fold from a Ten or a King. Checking back is the best way to reach a showdown and potentially win against missed spade draws or smaller pairs.

8-6 offsuit is below my opening range from UTG. With a solid 61 BB stack and 40 hands remaining, I should play tight and disciplined from early position. This weak, unconnected hand has poor equity and limited draw potential. Folding preserves my stack for premium hands where I have positional and equity advantages.

On the button with 6s4d — a weak, marginal hand but great position and short-term steal opportunity. Pot is 150 and the cost to defend is 100; rather than just calling and seeing a multiway flop with a poor hand, I’ll attempt a standard button steal. A raise to 300 is a common, table-consistent sizing that applies pressure to the blinds and will pick up the pot often. I know Elon (BB) can be aggressive and may 3-bet big — if he 3-bets aggressively I’ll fold this holding; if he (or SB) simply calls I’ll play postflop cautiously: c-bet on favorable low/paired/connected flops where I have equity or fold equity, check and control the pot on dry high-card boards. Overall this raise maximizes long-term EV by leveraging position and fold equity while keeping risk controlled with a weak offsuit hand.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind. Minni, on the button, has opened for a standard 3x raise. AQo is a premium hand in a 4-handed game and should almost always be played aggressively here to capitalize on my hand strength and take control of the pot. By 3-betting, I aim to either win the pot immediately or isolate Minni, while also making it difficult for Elon in the Big Blind to enter with a marginal hand. A raise to 1000 (roughly 3.3x her raise) is a standard size from out of position.

Pocket Jacks is one of the best starting hands, roughly top 3-4% of hands. Minni opened from button, which is typically wide, and Flash 3-bet from small blind to 1000, which can be polarized (strong value or bluffs/light 3-bets due to position). JJ is well ahead of Flash's calling range and crushes most button opens. With deep stacks (mine 19k, Minni 9k, Flash 4.3k), a 4-bet to 3000 (3x the 3-bet size) applies maximum pressure on Flash's shorter stack, isolates vs Minni if called, and builds a pot when ahead. Postflop, I have position on Flash (acts first). Folding or calling is suboptimal: fold too tight, call plays OOP multiway poorly. Raising maximizes EV.

Fold. After opening to 300 I face a 3-bet to 1,000 and a 4-bet to 3,000. My hand (6♠4♦) has very poor playability and reversed implied odds against this action. Calling 2,700 into a 4,300 pot requires ~38.7% equity to break even — my equity versus a polarized 4‑bet range (QQ+/AK, maybe some bluffs) is far below that. Even with position postflop, committing ~30% of my 8,950 stack with such a weak offsuit hand is low EV. A shove as a bluff is too high variance and unlikely to work often enough here. Preserve chips for better spots and fold preflop.

Elon's 4-bet from the Big Blind after my 3-bet is an extremely strong signal, especially since he is cold 4-betting into both the original raiser (Minni) and the 3-bettor (me). While Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium hand in a 4-handed game, it performs poorly against a standard 4-bet range, which typically consists of JJ+, AK, and occasional bluffs. By calling or jamming, I would be committing my entire remaining stack of 3300 chips. Given that my equity against a value-heavy 4-bet range is likely between 30-35% and the pot odds don't quite justify a call for my tournament life (or stack survival) in this cash game format, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my 33 BBs for a more favorable situation.

Fold. UTG in a 4-handed 50/100 game is first to act and will be out of position versus three opponents. 5♥3♦ offsuit is a very weak, poorly connected, unsuited holding that is routinely dominated by typical open/defending ranges. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives roughly 28.6% equity needed, and multiway equity with 5-3o is well below that; open-raising would build a pot OOP and likely get 3-bet or called by stronger hands. With ~89bb behind I should preserve stack and wait for a better spot (position or a playable holding). Fold to the blind.

I am on the button with 8c 3h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Minni has already folded. Although I am in position, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand, especially against two active players in the blinds. I will fold.

Preflop in SB vs BB only after UTG and Button folded. 6d7h is a speculative connector with straight potential, suitable for a wide SB steal range (50-60% hands standard). Raising to steal blinds or play postflop OOP with initiative. 3x BB size (300 total) is standard aggression here. Claude has 6k stack, but fold equity high. Avoid limping OOP with this hand to deny equity realization.

K2o is a weak hand with poor kicker. Facing aggression from Elon while short stacked (6000 chips) and out of position makes this a straightforward fold. I should preserve chips for premium spots rather than play marginal holdings against a larger stack in a heads-up situation.
I am in the UTG position with 6c 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Following standard tight-aggressive play, this is an easy fold in any position, especially UTG.

Preflop on the button with A5s after UTG fold. Excellent position with a suited ace that has strong playability, nut flush draw potential, and acts as an ace blocker. Standard profitable spot to raise for value/thin value against limpers or steals blinds. Players have shown aggression historically but stacks are deep enough for postflop play. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is optimal size to build pot or take it down now. Pot odds good, position advantage huge.

Q3o is a weak hand to get involved with from the small blind out of position. While I get pot odds of 1.8:1 (250 to call into 450), Q3 has poor equity against Elon's button raise range and I'll be out of position postflop. Elon has shown aggression from the button throughout the session and has a large stack (20450). This is a standard fold spot. I've been making disciplined laydowns with weak holdings and should continue that approach to preserve my 5950 chip stack.

6♠3♣ is garbage to defend out of position against a button open. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires roughly 31% equity, and 6-3o has well under that against a typical BTN opening range; postflop we'll be OOP facing frequent continuation bets. A min 3-bet to 500 would be a very thin bluff vs Elon's deep stack and aggressive tendencies and likely puts me in a losing spot if called or 4-bet. Preserve my 8,850 stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

87o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability from UTG position. Multi-way pots are likely, and it lacks strong equity or domination potential. Folding preserves our deep stack (20,900) for better spots, adhering to tight-aggressive strategy to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

Raising to 300 (3x BB) from the button after everyone else has folded. This is a standard button steal with a reasonable hand (Qs-Td). High fold equity against the blinds, and if called, I maintain positional advantage post-flop. Good stack-to-bet ratio, and the aggressive action is justified by my table position.

Fold. Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button, I'm in the small blind with 6♠5♥ offsuit — a marginal, poorly playable hand out of position. Calling 250 more to see a flop means I will be first to act postflop against an aggressive opener (and still have the big blind to act), and I can easily face c-bets or 3-bets in uncomfortable spots. A 3-bet or squeeze with this hand is too speculative; preserving my 88bb stack for clearer spots is the +EV play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 4d. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand is suited, 9-4 is quite weak and easily dominated. Calling 200 more to play a pot out of position with such a marginal hand is generally not profitable in the long run, even with the decent pot odds provided. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folding 7h 5h from UTG. This hand is too weak from early position with no positional advantage. With 6100 chips, I should be selective and wait for premium hands. UTG requires a tight opening range - 7-5 suited doesn't meet that threshold despite the small cost to call. Preserving stack and maintaining tight image for better spots is optimal strategy.

On the button with 5♦7♣ I have a weak, unconnected offsuit hand, but I'm last to act preflop with both blinds to come. Against two blind defenders I want to apply pressure and take advantage of position and fold equity rather than limp-call a marginal hand. A standard 3x open (300 total) gives decent fold equity and avoids seeing a cheap flop out of position; if I get called I’ll proceed cautiously — c-bet selectively on favorable flops, fold to strong resistance without a real piece, and always respect a large 3-bet (fold 5‑7o to significant 3-bet). Long-term this spot is worth opening frequently; with this particular hand I’ll make it a steal attempt sized to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. K6o is a weak starting hand to play out of position against a button open, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky given the current stack sizes and Minni's active playstyle. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision here.

Facing a button raise to 300 from Minni after SB folds. My hand 7d9c is offsuit, gapped, and weak with poor post-flop playability and low equity against a wide button opening range. Pot odds are 200 to call into 450 (~2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), but 79o realizes far less against typical calling or 3-betting ranges. With a deep effective stack vs Minni's 8500, folding preserves chips for better spots in BB defense strategy. No reads from history change this; button raisers are loose, but this hand doesn't defend profitably.

UTG with 8♣3♦ — a very weak, unsuited, poorly connected hand. This is early position with three opponents to act (Button, SB, BB) and the pot is just the blinds (150). Limping or opening with 8-3o is outside standard UTG ranges and will frequently get dominated or put me in difficult multiway situations. I have a healthy stack and should conserve chips for playable spots. Folding is the standard, low-variance play here.

I am on the button with K-4 offsuit. The action has folded to me. While King-high has some value, K-4 is generally too weak to open raise from the button in a 4-handed game unless the blinds are playing very tight. Elon and Claude have both shown aggression in previous hands. Raising here risks a 3-bet or a call from better hands, and the small stack relative to Elon doesn't provide much leverage. However, in most 4-max configurations, standard strategy suggests folding the weakest kings from the button. Given the current game flow, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop, SB vs BB after UTG and Button folds. 67o is a playable hand in this spot with position advantage postflop and deep stacks. Standard SB steal range includes offsuit connectors like this for fold equity against BB and playability if called. Raising standard size of 3x BB (300 total) to build pot or take it down now. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pot OOP.

3-Jack offsuit is a very weak hand that doesn't warrant defending in the big blind against Elon's raise. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to call into 400), my hand has minimal equity against a raiser who has shown consistent aggression throughout the session. Elon has a large stack (20500) and position advantage post-flop. With marginal equity and unfavorable circumstances, folding is the optimal play to preserve my 6000-chip stack. This is consistent with tight, solid big blind play.

In the UTG position with K5o, I am holding a hand that is generally too weak to open in a four-handed game. My stack is relatively short (around 41.5 big blinds), so I want to be selective with my opening range to avoid getting into difficult spots. This hand doesn't have much post-flop playability and easily runs into stronger King-x hands or better. Folding is the standard play here.

AJo is a strong hand from the button in a 4-handed game, especially after UTG folds. Position is excellent for value and stealing blinds. Opponents have shown aggressive tendencies but with short stacks relative to mine (Claude 5950, Minni 8850). Standard raise size of 3x BB (300) builds the pot when called or takes it down immediately. Calling is too passive; folding is too weak.

Small Blind with 8-J offsuit against Elon's button raise. While this is a marginal hand, the pot odds are favorable - I need to call 250 more into a 450-chip pot. 8-J has decent equity against a button raiser's typical range and can play well post-flop in position after seeing what happens. The call is mathematically justified given the small additional investment required and the hand's reasonable potential. This is a standard small blind defense spot against a frequent raiser.

T5o is a weak, unsuited hand and is very likely dominated by the button’s opening range and the small blind’s call. This is a multiway, out-of-position spot against two players; I’d need roughly 22% equity to justify the 200 call (200/(700+200)), and T5o’s realized equity multiway is well below that with poor implied odds. Raising as a bluff here is unattractive and I don’t want to get involved postflop out of position with a marginal hand, so I fold and preserve my ~88BB stack for better situations.

Out of position with J8 on a dry flop (2-6-9). No made hand and limited equity. Checking is the prudent play - it allows me to see Elon's continuation bet decision, keeps costs minimal, and maintains positional awareness. If he bets, I can fold without sunk costs. If he checks, the hand remains alive for two more cards. Betting J8 high out of position against an aggressive raiser would be speculatively weak.

Preflop: AJo is a premium hand from the button, raised standard 3x to 300, got called by Claude in SB (loose call range). Flop: 2d-6h-9d is a decent board for my range - low, somewhat drawy with diamond flush draw possible, but my ace-high is likely ahead of Claude's calling range (pairs, suited connectors, Ax weaker). He checks, so continuation bet to charge draws, protect equity, build pot, and deny equity to floats/weak hands. Position allows control. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (450) balances value/protection without overbetting. Checking back risks giving free cards to catch up.

Folding J8 out of position against Elon's c-bet on 2-6-9. No draws, limited equity against his button range, and poor position makes this a clear fold. Preserving stack for better spots with 34 hands remaining.

UTG position in a 4-handed game requires a tight opening range. Kc5c is suited but king-high with a weak kicker and low card, making it vulnerable to domination by better kings and poor post-flop playability against typical calling ranges. Folding preserves chips for better spots, especially deep-stacked at 213BB effective. Opponents show aggressive tendencies but no need to speculate here.

I'm holding 8d-Jc on the button after Elon folded pre-flop. This is a solid hand in late position with good equity against the blinds. Calling the 100 to complete the big blind is correct here because:

1. Position advantage: I'm on the button with last action post-flop, which is valuable
2. Hand strength: 8-J offsuit is a respectable hand that can flop well (straight draws, pair draws, etc.)
3. Stack depth: My 5700 stack is reasonable, and calling here keeps flexibility
4. Pot odds: Getting nearly 2:1 on a call makes this easy to play for a potentially profitable hand
5. Image: I've been relatively tight (many folds pre-flop), so a button call fits my table dynamics

I'll call and use position to play profitably post-flop.

2♥6♣ is junk — not something I want to raise or play aggressively out of the small blind. However the price to see a flop is tiny: I’ve already posted 50 and calling 50 more completes to 100 into a 250 pot (good pot odds). I’ll call to see a cheap flop and then fold to meaningful aggression unless I pair or pick up a realistic draw. If the flop is checked to me I’ll proceed cautiously (check/call small bets, fold to large bets).

I am in the Big Blind with K♥ 9♦. The action has been limped by Claude on the Button and Minni in the Small Blind. K9o is a decent hand, but there is no need to bloat the pot out of position against two opponents. I will check to see a free flop and evaluate from there.

I called preflop for a cheap look. Flop Q♠ A♣ A♠ and I hold 2♥6♣ — no pair, no realistic draw, and I’m out of position against two players who already showed willingness to call preflop (likely range contains Ax/Qx and hands that connect). There’s no value to betting and bluffing here is thin because this board heavily hits opponents’ call ranges. I'll check and concede to any meaningful bet (per my preflop plan I fold unimproved junk), then only continue if I pair or pick up a legitimate draw on later streets.

In a limped pot on a flop of Qs-Ac-As, I have King-high with no draw. This board heavily favors whoever might have an Ace or a Queen. Since Minni checked, I will also check to see if Claude (the button) bets. Betting here is unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Queen, and I have very little equity to continue if raised or called.

I have 8d-Jc on a flop of Qs-As-Ac—essentially Jack-high with no equity. Both opponents checked, indicating likely weakness, but a paired Ace board means I'm rarely ahead. With a 5600 chip stack, burning chips with a bluff here is counterproductive. I check to keep the pot small and preserve chips, with the intention to fold if there's significant action. My position advantage is negated by my poor hand strength, so pot control is the priority.

I called preflop for a cheap look with 2♥6♣ and per my plan I only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw. The board A♣ A♠ Q♠ 7♣ completely misses my hand — no pair, no meaningful draw, and I’m out of position to two players (Button and BB) who showed willingness to call preflop. Betting here would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity and poor blockers; folding to any meaningful bet is my default. Preserve chips for better spots and check down.

In this limped pot, the board shows a pair of Aces and a Queen. Everyone checked the flop, indicating a likely lack of strength across the board. The turn 7 is a blank for most ranges. While I have King-high, which could potentially be the best hand if everyone else is also holding air, there is little value in betting here into two opponents. If I bet, I'm likely only called by better hands (like a Queen or a low pocket pair) and I don't need to protect my hand against much. I'll check and see if the Button (Claude) decides to take a stab at it, or if we can see a cheap river.

I'm holding 8d-Jc on a Qs-Ac-As-7c board with Jack-high. This is an extremely weak holding on a paired Ace board. Both opponents checked the flop and turn, indicating they likely don't have strong holdings or are slowplaying Aces.

My best strategy is pot control:
1. My hand has minimal equity and essentially zero showdown value
2. Betting here is counterproductive - I'd either get called by something better or fold out weaker hands I could beat
3. Both opponents' checks suggest they're not confident in their hands
4. I have 5600 chips and don't want to waste them bluffing into a paired board
5. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply and potentially win at showdown if both opponents miss

This is a clear check. If either opponent bets on the river, I fold. If both check again, I go to showdown.

Check. I called preflop for a cheap look and never improved — 2♥6♣ is dead on this runout. The river completed a paired board (A♣ A♠ Q♠ 7♣ 7♠) that heavily favors opponents’ calling ranges (Ax, 7x, Qx, small pairs, or floats). With two players left the fold equity for a river bluff is very low, and any bet is unlikely to fold out the aces, sevens, or queens that beat me while getting called by a lot of worse hands. The pot is small relative to my stack; there’s no value to extracting chips and too much downside to bluff. Preserve the stack for better spots — check and go to showdown/muck.

The board is Ac-As-Qs-7c-7s. This provides a two pair (Aces and Sevens) on the board with a Queen kicker. My best hand using Kh-9d is two pair (Aces and Sevens) with a King kicker. Since a King is higher than a Queen, my current hand (As-Ac-7s-7c-Kh) is better than the board's standing hand (As-Ac-7s-7c-Qs). However, any player holding an Ace (for Aces full of Sevens), a Seven (for Sevens full of Aces), or a Queen (for Aces and Queens) has me beaten. Given that the action has been checked through every street, it is possible everyone has very weak holdings. However, there is no value in betting here; I won't be called by worse hands, and I'm unlikely to fold out a Queen or better in a pot this small. I will check and take it to showdown.

I'm holding 8d-Jc on the river with the board showing Qs-Ac-As-7c-7s. My hand is Jack-high with no equity on a paired Ace board. Both opponents (Minni and Flash) checked through the flop and turn, and now both have checked to me on the river, indicating they likely don't have strong holdings or are content with their weak hands.

Betting here makes no sense:
1. If I bet and they fold, I win only 300 chips from the pot
2. If I bet and they call, I lose to a better hand (any pair, Ace, or possibly a Queen)
3. My Jack-high has zero showdown value against any real hand

Given that both players checked the river, going to showdown is the optimal play. I'll check and see what happens. With my previous history of cautious play on this hand (checked flop and turn), checking the river is consistent with my line and maximizes my remaining stack for future hands.

QJ is a solid broadway hand that warrants a raise from UTG. Standard 3x BB sizing to 300 demonstrates strength, takes initiative, and builds the pot with a quality holding. With my stack size and position, this is the value-oriented play.

Claude opened UTG to 300. I’m on the button with 6♠3♥ — a trash, highly dominated hand with very poor playability and little implied odds. The pot is 450; calling 300 would require ~40% equity to break even (300/(450+300)), which 6-3o does not have versus an UTG opening range (true equity well under that). A light 3‑bet/squeeze from me is possible in some spots, but with this weak offsuit hand against an early-position raiser and likely action from the blinds, it’s not a profitable spot. Preserve stack and wait for a better opportunity — fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 300 from the UTG position. Calling out of position with such a weak hand against a pre-flop raiser is generally a losing strategy, especially with the Big Blind still to act. I will fold.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 300 into a 450 pot, needing 200 to call from BB. My KJo offsuit is a strong hand in position with excellent postflop playability, high card strength, straight potential, and position. Deep stacks (my 21k vs Claude's 5.3k), but opponents behind have folded. Standard play is to 3-bet for value/isolation/take it down, as KJo has good equity vs UTG opening range and blocks AA/KK. Sizing to 900 total (3x the raise), which is standard and commits ~17% of Claude's stack while building the pot.

Folding QJ to Elon's 3-bet. While the pot odds are reasonable (33% needed vs 40-45% equity), the factors that make this call unfavorable are: (1) Playing out of position heads-up against a bigger stack with superior position, (2) Elon's range likely skews stronger than my marginal holding, (3) Stack depth disparity (I have 5300 vs his 20400) makes post-flop decisions difficult, (4) I'll be forced to navigate a multi-street hand in a weak position. In cash games, disciplined folding preserves chips for better spots where I have better position/equity dynamics.

UTG with K♦6♦ is marginal, but this is a 4‑handed game so opening ranges are wider than at a full table. Limping (calling 100) just invites multiway action and gets my weak king into tough spots; folding is too tight. I want to seize the initiative, get fold equity and define the pot — standard open sizing here is 3x the big blind (300). I will raise to 300. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (preserve stack vs heavy aggression), otherwise proceed postflop with continuation bets on favourable textures and pot‑control when I miss.

I am on the button with 8h 4h. Minni, who has been quite active and often opens for 300, has raised to 300 from UTG. While my hand is suited, 8-4 is very weak and lacks high-card value. My stack is relatively short (43bb), and calling a 3x open with this hand puts me in a difficult spot post-flop, especially with the blinds yet to act. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
