He checks, representing weakness or traps, but given his position and stack, likely weak. I have position and a large stack advantage (22BB+ deep vs his 35BB). No direct equity (gutshot to 10-J but thin), but backdoor spade and high cards for float/turn play. Optimal to c-bet ~60% pot (400) as a balanced continuation: value with any pair/draw, bluff with air to deny equity/charge draws/fold weak. Takes down often vs checked range, builds pot if called. Folding or checking suboptimal as it cedes initiative postflop. Near session end, apply pressure to chip up vs short stack.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six of diamonds. On a flop of 8h-4h-3d, I have King-high and a backdoor diamond draw. Elon has bet 400 into a 650 pot. This is a fairly standard continuation bet from a Button raiser, but my hand lacks any direct equity like a pair or a straight/flush draw. Calling out of position with just King-high against an aggressive player with a significant chip lead is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and preserve my remaining 35 big blinds for a better opportunity.

Calling A5o from the button with 3:2 pot odds and superior position. The hand has ace-high potential and the small call size is well-justified by position advantage in 3-handed play. I'll look to see the flop and leverage my positional edge post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The Button (Claude) has limped in for 100. With an Ace in my hand and being relatively short-stacked (about 35 big blinds), completing the bet is a standard play to see a flop cheaply. Raising is an option to isolate, but given Elon's aggressive history in the Big Blind, I prefer to keep the pot small and realize my equity.

Preflop in the big blind with 9s5s, a suited gapper with some straight and flush potential but mediocre high-card strength. Pot is 300 after blinds (my 100 posted), Button limp from Claude (likely wide range), and SB Flash completes cheaply (short stack at 3425, also wide). Facing no additional call (to call: 0), checking is optimal to realize equity multiway in position on the flop cheaply. Raising risks isolating with a marginal hand against short stack or getting called wide, better to pot control and play postflop with deep effective stacks (Flash shortest). Consistent with GTO vs limpers in BB.

On a flop of Qd-3h-Kh, I have Ace-high with a weak kicker. This board hits many Broadway cards that both Claude and Elon could have in their limping/checking ranges. Leading out into two opponents with no significant equity or draw is ill-advised. I will check to see how the action develops and will likely fold to any significant bet, as Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand here against two players.

Flop Qd 3h Kh rainbow-ish with two hearts. Holding 9s5s: no pair, no made straight draw (gutshot unlikely), backdoor spade flush draw and straight potential, but very weak overall with 9-high. Preflop limpers suggest wide ranges from Claude (button) and Flash (SB short stack at 34BB). Flash checked first, pot 300, deep effective vs Claude but Flash short. Betting risks folding out better equity realization or getting called by better; checking keeps pot small multiway, realizes any thin equity on turn/river cheaply in position post-check (BB acts last postflop). Consistent with pot control and GTO vs limpers: check marginal hands to showdown or improve. No strong blockers to KQ, avoid thin value. Opponent tendencies from history show frequent checking/showdowns.

Betting the flop after both opponents check. I have position and ace-high, which may be the best hand. Both players showed weakness by checking, making this an ideal spot to apply pressure and take down the pot or win with the best hand. A bet of 200 (2/3 of the 300-chip pot) is a standard flop continuation bet that demonstrates strength without overextending with a marginal holding. This is exploitative poker in 3-handed play where position is extremely valuable.

The flop is King-Queen-Three, which heavily favors the pre-flop limper's and the big blind's ranges. I have only Ace-high with a five kicker and no real drawing potential. Claude has bet 200 into a 300 pot, and Elon is still to act behind me. Given my weak absolute and relative strength on this board, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

Facing a 200 bet into a 500 pot on Qd3hKh flop with 9s5s (9-high, no pair, no straight draw, weak backdoor spade draw). Preflop multiway limped pot, checks from Flash (short SB) and me induce Claude's (Button) wide limp range to bet for value (KQ, Kx, Qx, pairs, draws) or thin value. Equity crushed vs betting range (~5-10% equity max, per solvers), pot odds 2.5:1 insufficient even if optimistic. Calling commits to further tough spots OOP vs aggressive Button continuation; folding preserves stack for better spots in late game (hand 140/150). Consistent with prior pot control check and GTO: fold marginal air post-check. History shows Claude bets flops selectively after limps.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with Queen-Five offsuit. While button opening ranges are generally wide in short-handed play, Q5o is at the bottom of that range. Given my current stack of roughly 34 big blinds, I am the short stack compared to Elon and Claude. Playing a marginal hand like this against larger stacks who have shown aggression in previous hands is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations or losing chips to a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining stack.

T6d suited from SB vs BB only after Button folds. Deep stacks allow for implied odds with suited connector potential for straights, flushes, two-pair. Limping is standard here to realize equity cheaply out of position rather than folding (too tight) or raising (too strong for marginal hand). Pot odds excellent: adding 50 more to 150 pot (3:1). Plan to play post-flop based on board texture.

Heads-up against Elon who limped from the small blind. His call suggests weakness. With A6o, I'm likely ahead of his range. Raising to 300 (betting 200 more) applies pressure and builds the pot. This is a standard aggressive play in heads-up when the opponent shows weakness through a limp. It wins the pot immediately against some holdings and builds value when ahead.

Suited connector Td6d in SB facing BB iso-raise to 300 after my limp. Deep stacks (23k effective vs Claude's 12k), pot 400 to call 200 more (2:1 odds, need ~33% equity). T6d has strong implied odds and playability postflop: flush/straight potential, good vs wide BB range (22+, A9s+, KTs+, QJs+, AJo+, KQo etc.) where it has ~40-45% equity. OOP challenge but small ball postflop viable. Folding too tight pre, 3bet/raise too strong for speculative hand. Call to realize equity cheaply.

Heads-up on flop OOP with Td6d on 4h-8c-9s rainbow board. Hand has gutshot straight draw (4 outs to 7 for 6789T), backdoor diamond flush draw, and T-high. Equity around 35-40% against Claude's wide BB iso-range (pairs 66+, suited connectors, broadways like KQo+, AJo+), strong implied odds with deep effective stacks (~12k). Board dry, favors his range slightly (hits sets, two-pair, overpairs, draws). No fold equity for a bet OOP as villain likely continues wide; betting risks check-raise or isolation without strong showdown value. Checking standard: pot control, induce bluffs, realize equity cheaply on turn if he checks back or bet small. Folding preposterous postflop with pot odds. No raise possible without bet. Plan: check, call small/medium bets, fold to large overbets.

Continuation betting on the flop after my pre-flop aggression. With A6o on 4h-8c-9s, I have ace-high which is currently the best hand against Elon's checking range. His check indicates weakness or caution. Betting 350 (about 58% of pot) continues my aggressive narrative, potentially wins the pot immediately, and keeps my range balanced. This is a standard aggressive play in heads-up when the opponent checks. Stack depths allow flexibility on future streets.

Facing a small 350 bet into 950 pot (~1/3 pot) on 4h8c9s flop OOP with Td6d. Hand has 4-outer gutshot to 7 for straight (6789T), backdoor diamond flush draw, and overcard T. Strong playability and ~30-40% equity vs Claude's wide BB iso-range over limp (88+, 97s+, 87s+, 76s+, broadway, Ax suited). Pot odds excellent: 950:350 (~2.7:1, ~27% needed). Deep effective stacks (12.5k Claude) offer huge implied odds if we hit. Raising OOP risks check-raise or heads-up with worse position; folding too tight preflop investment. Calling realizes equity cheaply, controls pot, induces bluffs on turns/rivers, sets up check-raise if strong turn. Plan: call, check turn unless major improvement, evaluate bet sizing.

Turn board pairs the 4d, completing no straight for our gutshot (still 4 outs to 7), adds a diamond giving us additional backdoor flush draw potential (3 diamonds needed with Td6d), but no direct improvement to made hand. Board now 4h8c9s4d favors villain's BB iso + cbet range heavily: pairs 88+, 97s+, sets, two-pair combos like 98/44, overpairs, and draws now less scary post-pair. Equity ~25-35% OOP vs his range (stronger now with paired board protecting value). Deep effective stacks (12.5k Claude), but pot control crucial OOP with no showdown value beyond T-high and speculative draws. Betting OOP risks raising into better hands or folding out equity; no fold equity likely as villain barrels wide. Checking standard: induces bets from value/draws we can realize equity against, allows check-call/raise if overbet, or check-check to cheap river. Folding preposterous with investment (900+ in pot for us). Plan: check, evaluate villain's sizing/response for river decision, fold to large bets if no further improvement.

Checking the turn on 4h-8c-9s-4d with A6. Elon has shown weakness by checking twice, indicating his hand is marginal. My ace-high is vulnerable and shouldn't face a turn bet against checked equity. Checking preserves pot control, avoids overextending a marginal hand, and allows me to see the river cheaply with potential to improve. If another A hits the river, I'll have a strong hand to extract value. This is a standard pot control play in heads-up when holding weak ace-high on a paired board against an opponent showing weakness.

River Ace on a paired 4h8c9s4d board completes strong hands like Ax (which hit top pair/top kicker or better), sets, two-pairs (44xx, 48s, 49s, 84s, 94s, 88/99), and straights (5-6-7-8-9 possible with 567). Td6d remains T-high with no pair, straight (missed gutshot to 7), or flush. Equity <20% vs Claude's BB iso + flop cbet + turn checkback range (value-heavy: pairs 88+, overpairs QQ+, Ax suited/broadway, draws that missed). Deep stacks but no implied odds left on river OOP. Betting pure air as bluff bad: poor fold equity OOP vs player who cbets flops/checks turns (polarized/weak or value), blocks no value (no A/T), faces raises from better. Checking standard: pot control with no showdown value (loses to any pair/Ace), induces bluffs from missed draws (76s, 57s, flush draws that bricked), cheap showdown if check-check. Plan: check, fold to 1/2+ pot bets (no fold equity if raised pre-turn), hero-call only vs tiny bet (unlikely from villain). Consistent with small-ball speculative preflop strategy but folding equity realization on blank river.

Value betting on the river with pair of Aces. Elon's passive line (checked turn and river after calling two streets) indicates a weak/marginal holding. The river Ace completes a strong hand for me while remaining consistent with my aggressive pre-flop and flop betting line. Betting 600 (~46% of pot) extracts value from weaker pairs and whiffed draws while applying reasonable pressure. This is a standard value extraction play when ahead with a made hand against a passive opponent showing weakness.

River Ac on 4h8c9s4d board completes Ax for TPK (Claude's BB iso + flop cbet + turn checkback + river bet range heavily favors value: A8s/A9s/ATs/AA, 88/99/44 trips, two-pair 84/94/48/49), straights like 567, and improves overpairs QQ+/KK. Td6d is pure T-high bluff-catcher with 0 showdown vs any pair/Ace, missed gutshot/backdoors. Pot odds 1900:600 (~3.2:1, ~24% equity needed) but realized equity <10-15% vs polarized/value-heavy range (bluffs like missed 57s/67s/76s/T7s unlikely at 1/3 pot sizing; villain cbets flop/checks turn then bets small river suggests medium strength/value thin/protection). Deep stacks irrelevant on river OOP with no further implied odds. Hero-call spewy: loses to value, beaten by better bluffs even. History shows Claude value-bets rivers (e.g., Hand 137 river bet called by value). Folding preserves stack for better spots near end of 150-hand session, consistent with small-ball limp/call preflop speculative hand that whiffed entirely.

3-handed on the button with SB folded, facing lone BB who has 33BB. T6s is a strong speculative hand with suited connectors, good playability, straight/flush potential. Position is huge advantage. Standard play is to raise for value, initiative, and fold equity. BB may defend wide but we have postflop edge. Raising to 300 (3x BB) fits my aggressive preflop image from history and maximizes EV.

Premium pocket pair (KK) in small blind after Elon's button raise. Standard 3-bet to build value with the second-best starting hand. Raise sizing of 750 (500 additional) is approximately 2.5x Elon's raise, which is standard. This shows strength, builds the pot while ahead, and tests Elon's holding. My 13,750 stack is healthy enough to support this aggression, and Elon's larger stack suggests he can call if he has a strong hand. Flash's short stack (3,325) means he may soon be forced all-in regardless.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Elon raised from the button to 300, and Claude three-bet from the small blind to 750. This is a very strong signal from Claude, especially since it's a three-bet out of the small blind against a button open. Queen-Nine offsuit is not a hand that plays well against a three-bet range, and I am out of position against both players. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and four-betting would be over-bluffing with a hand that has mediocre playability. The best decision here is to fold and preserve my remaining 33.25 big blinds for better opportunities in the final hands of the game.

Facing a 3-bet from Claude in the SB after my button open with T6o, a marginal offsuit connector with poor equity against a typical 3-bet range (which includes strong Ax, broadway, pockets). Pot is 1150, calling 450 gives ~2.6:1 odds, but postflop playability is limited without suitedness or better connectivity, and I risk getting dominated. Position helps, but folding preserves my deep stack (22475) for better spots with 9 hands left. No strong blockers for a 4-bet bluff. Consistent with GTO: fold weak opens to 3-bets. Maintain aggressive image by folding correctly here.

Raising with A4o on the button is standard play. Flash's critically short stack (3,275) makes this an ideal situation - I'm either winning the blinds or getting all-in with decent equity against his range. Elon's large stack makes his call less likely. Position advantage and proper sizing support a raise here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. This is a standard late-position open. My hand is strong enough to defend, but because I have a relatively short stack (32.75 big blinds) and will be out of position for the rest of the hand, calling is often better than 3-betting unless I want to commit. However, against a Button raise, A7o is a solid candidate to see a flop. Given my stack size and position, calling is a reasonable defensive play to see if I hit an Ace or a favorable board without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

3-handed, Button (Claude) raises to 2.5BB (250), SB (Flash) calls. K8o is a marginal hand OOP multiway. Pot odds are 600:150 (4:1, ~20% needed), but K8o likely dominated by better kings and pairs in ranges, poor postflop playability OOP multiway. Button range wide but SB call caps it somewhat. No strong blockers or suited. Deep stack, no need to speculate. Standard fold to preserve stack.

I have Ace-high on a dry board with a Queen and a Nine. As the pre-flop caller in the Small Blind, the standard play is to check to the initial aggressor (Claude on the Button) to see how he reacts. I don't have a made hand, so leading into the raiser would be an unnecessary risk with my current stack size.

With A4o on a 2h-Qc-9d flop, I have a weak ace-high hand vulnerable to many holdings Flash could have. Since Flash checked to me, indicating weakness, checking preserves my stack and maintains flexibility. Position advantage is better leveraged by letting Flash act on subsequent streets rather than committing more chips with marginal equity. This also keeps the option to win with showdown value or fold if Flash demonstrates strength on later streets.

On the turn, the board is 2h-Qc-9d-8c. After checking the flop and seeing the button check back, my Ace-high still has some showdown value, but the 8c on the turn introduces some straight possibilities (J-10, 10-7) and more mid-range hits for my opponent. However, since the button checked behind on the flop, their range is unlikely to contain many strong Queens or top-tier hands. Leading here doesn't accomplish much as a bluff and might get called by better hands. Checking again is the most prudent play to try and get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff that I can evaluate on the river.

Checking preserves stack with vulnerable ace-high against a critically short-stacked opponent who has shown weakness with two checks. Position advantage is better utilized by letting Flash act first on the river, where I can make better decisions with more information about board completion and final card texture.

I have Ace-high on a board that has become quite connected (Q-J-9-8-2). Claude raised from the button but checked both the flop and the turn, which caps his range significantly. While my Ace-high has almost no showdown value against his likely range (small pairs, weak Nines, or even a Jack that just hit), betting into a pot that has been checked down twice is risky as he might call with any pair. I will check and hope for a check-back or a very cheap showdown, though I am prepared to fold if he bets given the coordination of the board.

River check preserves my marginal ace-high against a weak range that's shown consistent strength throughout (two flop/turn checks, river check). Flash's small 3,075 remaining stack and checking pattern indicate he doesn't have a strong hand worth extracting value from. Betting risks losing to an unseen better hand with minimal upside - Flash likely checks down air or folds with nothing. Going to showdown maximizes my chances of winning a pot against a wide weak range, minimizes losses if Flash has an underpair or straight, and avoids burning chips on the river with poor equity.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While it's a marginal hand, the blinds are quite high and I have a relatively short stack compared to the others. Raising to steal the blinds is a viable option, but since I am the short stack and want to minimize risk in the final hands, folding this weak hand is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my remaining chips.

4d8c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the SB vs BB. No strong equity or straight/flush potential against BB's defending range. Pot odds are 50 to win 150 (3:1), but post-flop implied odds are minimal with this trash hand. Deep stacks don't justify speculative calling here. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for premium spots.

3-handed on the button with T4 suited. This is a strong speculative hand with straight and flush potential, excellent playability multiway or heads-up. Position is ideal for realizing equity. Pot has 150 from blinds (likely SB folded or limped minimally), to call 100. Raising steals dead money with fold equity against blinds, especially short-stacked Flash (~33BB, may shove but we have blockers and can play postflop). Calling risks multiway OOP; folding too tight late in tournament. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300 for initiative and pot control.

Folding 9-7 offsuit from the small blind. This is a weak hand that doesn't play well against aggressive opponents like Elon, especially out of position. With only 6 hands remaining and a healthy stack (14,250), there's no reason to gamble with marginal holdings. Preserving chips for better spots in the final hands is the correct endgame strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with A5o. Elon, who has been aggressive pre-flop throughout the game, opens from the button to 300. Given the late-game situation (Hand 145/150) and my relatively short stack of 32.75 big blinds (3275 chips), A5o is a reasonable hand to defend against a wide button opening range. However, calling out of position is often difficult. Looking at Elon's history, he frequently continues on the flop. A 3-bet would be a high-variance move given my stack size. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my Ace-high strength or potential low-pair value. I will call the additional 200 to see the flop.

I have completely missed the flop on a board (Tc-8s-Qc) that is very favorable for Elon's button opening range, which includes many broadway cards and connectors. With just Ace-high and no real draws, I have very little equity here. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and plan to fold if he bets, as I cannot profitably continue with this hand out of position on such a connected texture.

Flop Tc8sQc gives me top pair (tens) with a weak 4 kicker and backdoor diamond flush draw. Flash checked from BB after calling a raise pre-flop, indicating a wide range including weaker pairs, overcards, draws, or floats. The board is draw-heavy with two clubs (flush draw) and straight possibilities (9J). In position on button vs short-stacked opponent (~30BB), betting builds pot for value from worse hands (e.g., worse tens, nines, eights, Ax suited clubs) while providing protection against draws. Checking risks giving free cards to improve. Pot 650, bet sizing ~60% pot (400) balances value and fold equity without overbetting into a potential trap. Avoids check-raise all-in risk from short stack.

The flop texture of Tc-8s-Qc is highly coordinated and hits many of Elon's button-opening hands (like J9, KJ, QJ, AT, etc.). I have Ace-high with virtually no backdoor equity or draws. Elon has followed up his pre-flop aggression with a 400-chip bet into a 650-chip pot. With only 3075 chips remaining and a weak hand out of position, there is no profitable way to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final five hands.

7-9 offsuit is too weak to play even from button position with only 5 hands remaining. At 1.5:1 pot odds, the hand doesn't have sufficient equity to justify a call against reasonably strong ranges. Better to preserve stack for premium hands in the final 5 hands of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Clubs and Eight of Spades. Since the Button folded, it's a head-up situation between me and Elon in the Big Blind. A8o is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind.

Pocket 77 is a strong hand preflop, especially against a likely wide small blind raising range which includes many Ax, suited connectors, and broadway hands. I'm in the big blind with position advantage postflop heads-up, and Claude has folded. Flash's stack is 2775 (~28BB), so a 3-bet can build the pot or take it down preflop. Calling is fine for set mining with great pot odds (pot 400 to call 200), but 3-betting applies pressure, isolates, and denies equity to his worse hands. Standard 3-bet size to ~3x his raise (900 total). My deep stack covers this easily, and 77 performs well in 3-bet pots OOP vs SB range.

In a blind-vs-blind battle, ranges are significantly wider than in other positions. My Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand in this context. Elon, having a massive chip lead, is likely 3-betting with a wide range to capitalize on his stack and the late stage of the game. Calling the 600 chips gives me roughly 2-to-1 odds on the call, and it sets up a post-flop scenario with a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of approximately 1. This SPR allows for high flexibility to commit my stack on favorable boards or if I connect with an Ace or an Eight, while avoiding the potential of running into a dominant hand by 4-bet shoving pre-flop.

I have top pair with an Ace, but the board is extremely coordinated (Ah, Kh, Qs). Elon's 3-bet range pre-flop includes many hands that have me crushed, such as AK, AQ, AJ, KK, QQ, and JT. Given my remaining stack size and the high likelihood that Elon's range hit this board, I will check to see his action. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on such a wet board is risky.

Preflop 3-bet with 77 was standard to isolate SB open and apply pressure. However, this Ah Kh Qs flop is disastrous for my holding: pocket 77 misses completely, with no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw, and blocks nothing relevant against villain's strong 3-bet calling range (AKo/s, AQ, KQ, AA, KK, QQ, AQs, etc.). The board is highly coordinated with broadway combos, straight draws (JT), and heart flush draws. Flash checked (pot 1800), giving me first action OOP in this heads-up pot. Betting risks getting called/raised by his entire range while my bluff gets no value from worse hands he might check. Shoving ~2175 into 1800 commits ~120% pot but has zero equity realization against his caps; he'd call wide with top pair+, draws, and even some floats. Best play is check to pot control, realize equity for free if he checks behind, and fold to any bet (which he will do often with value or semi-bluffs). Preserves deep stack for better spots in late game (hand 146/150).

On the turn, the 2 of clubs is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. Both Elon and I checked the flop, which was extremely heavy with broadway cards (AKQ). Elon's flop check as the pre-flop 3-bettor suggests he either has a marginal hand he wants to take to showdown (like a weak Ace, King, or Queen) or he is potentially trapping with a monster. With my Ace-Eight, I have top pair, but my kicker is very weak on this specific board where many common 3-betting hands like AJ, AT, or even pocket pairs that hit sets (KK, QQ) or straights (JT) are in his range. Given the pot size and my remaining stack, I will check again to control the size of the pot and hope to get to a cheap showdown. If Elon bets, I will have to decide whether my top pair is good enough to call off my remaining chips, but for now, checking is the most cautious and strategic move.

Preflop 3-bet with 77 was to isolate the SB raiser and apply pressure, which got us heads-up. Flop AhKhQs is terrible for 77: complete whiff, no pair, draw, or relevant blockers against Flash's 3-bet calling range (strong broadway, pairs TT+, suited Ax/Kx/Qx, etc.). We checked flop OOP for pot control after his check. Turn 2c bricks, adding no help, and Flash checks again OOP, which could be weakness (missed draws like JT, suited connectors), trapping with monsters (sets, two-pair, straight), or floaters. Pot 1800, Flash has 2175 (~1.2x pot shove possible). Betting risks getting called/shoved on by his entire value range while bluffing poorly (he folds too little given SPR<2); no fold equity vs short stack. 77 has minimal showdown value (beats pure air/floats, but unlikely). Checking preserves stack, realizes equity for free on river, and avoids spew late in game (146/150) with deep stack. If he bets river, easy fold.

The river Jack of Clubs completes a very coordinated board (Ah-Kh-Qs-2c-Jc). Any Ten now has a straight. Since I am first to act and the hand has been checked down since the flop, my pair of Aces with an 8 kicker has showdown value but is extremely vulnerable to many hands in Elon's 3-bet range (like AJ, KJ, QJ, or any hand with a Ten). Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands and might be raised by better ones. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown.

Preflop 3-bet with 77 was standard to isolate SB open and pressure his wide range. Heads-up to flop AhKhQs: complete whiff for 77—no pair, draw, or blockers vs. Flash's strong 3-bet calling range (AK/AQ/KQ/AJ/KJ, sets TT+/pairs, suited broadways). Checked flop OOP for pot control after his check. Turn 2c changes nothing; checked again. River Jc completes no new draws (no broadway straight without 10, clubs flush unlikely given no prior club threat, hearts draw bricked). Board: Ah Kh Qs 2c Jc. Pot 1800, Flash has 2175 (~1.2x pot). His triple check OOP post-3bet call suggests possible weakness (missed draws like JT♥, suited connectors), floats, or traps (monsters like sets/AK). Betting/shoving has poor fold equity—short stack calls profitably with any pair/top pair+ (beats 77), value hands jam/call, bluffs unlikely given passive line. 77 has showdown value vs. air/missed draws he checks. Check realizes equity for free, avoids spew late (146/150) with deep stack. Preserves chips for final hands.

On the button in a 3-handed game with pocket fours, this is a standard opening hand. With only 4 hands left in the session, I want to play aggressively with pairs to either win the blinds or see a flop in position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate given my 40bb stack.

Js7s is a mediocre suited hand with some playability but significant gaps. Facing a button raise from Flash (36BB stack), range is wide, but I'm OOP in SB. Calling 250 into 450 pot OOP risks multiway pot with BB likely defending, and postflop play is difficult with this hand. 3-bet light possible but Flash short and can shove. Fold is best to preserve deep stack for better spots near end of 150 hands.

Folding T2o to Flash's button raise. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker with poor equity and weak kicker value. Even with favorable pot odds, this hand has negative expected value against Flash's raising range. Being in the big blind doesn't justify playing marginal hands - I should preserve my healthy stack of 14,150 for better opportunities.

3-handed on the button with KJo, a premium opening hand with good high-card strength, playability, and equity. First to act preflop, standard to raise for value and to steal blinds. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is a good size given the pot of 150 and opponents' stacks (Claude deep at 14100, Flash 4025 ~40BB). Builds pot when ahead, applies pressure.

Jh-4d is a weak hand without sufficient equity. I have a healthy 14,100-chip stack and can afford to be selective. Folding weak hands from early position allows me to preserve chips for premium situations in the final hands of this session. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Elon has raised from the Button to 300. This is a standard late-position steal attempt. My hand has decent high-card value and an Ace, making it a reasonable defend. Calling the 200 more into a pot of 450 gives me 3.25-to-1 odds. Against a wide button opening range, A5o is often ahead or has enough equity to see a flop. Folding would be too tight given the late-game situation (Hand 148 of 150) and my stack size, and 3-betting is risky given Elon's massive stack and my position. I will call to see the flop.

I have Ace-high (Ac 5h) on a flop of Ts 7s 2c. As the Big Blind who defended against a Button raise, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here (donk betting) is generally unfavorable as the board doesn't specifically favor my range over Elon's Button opening range. I will check and see how Elon reacts. If he bets small, I may consider calling based on my high-card value; if he checks behind, I get a free card to potentially improve on the turn.

3-handed on button after preflop raise vs BB call. Flop Ts7s2c is coordinated with spade flush draw, gutshots (e.g., 89, J9, 98), but somewhat dry. KJo blocks kings (possible top pair for Flash), jack for gutshots, and has overcard equity plus backdoor spade draw (Js). Flash checks, indicating possible weakness given BB defending range (suited broadways, connectors, Ax suited) often misses. As preflop aggressor in position, standard c-bet to deny equity to draws/improve hands, charge floats, and take down pot frequently. ~2/3 pot size (450 into 650) balances value from Ax/high cards and bluffs, given pot odds and Flash's 38BB stack. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws/OESD.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a flop of Ts 7s 2c. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor from the Button, has made a continuation bet of 450 into a pot of 650 (approx 70% pot). While Ace-high has some showdown value against a wide Button range, I have no significant draws on this board and am out of position against a very aggressive opponent with a massive chip lead. Calling here would likely force me to face another large bet on the turn, where most cards will not improve my hand. Given that we are at Hand 148 of 150 and I am the short stack, I need to conserve my chips for a more advantageous situation or a better semi-bluffing opportunity. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Folding 9-5 offsuit on the button. While position is an advantage, this hand is too weak to profitably call. With a decent stack of 14,100 and only two hands remaining, there's no compelling reason to play marginal hands. 9-5o has poor equity and limited post-flop potential. Preserving my stack for the final hand is the prudent decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four suited. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and Elon in the Big Blind. Q4s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-versus-blind situation, even though my stack is significantly smaller than Elon's. Raising to 300 is a standard play to take control of the pot or win the blinds immediately.

Hand #149, near the end of 150 hands. In BB with Th6s, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability OOP vs SB raise. Flash raised to 300 from SB (3x), standard steal attempt but their range is wide yet includes many better hands. Pot odds are 400:200 (3:1), but equity ~35% vs wide range, and postflop OOP with dominated hand risks spew. My stack is massive (21975 vs Flash's 3525), so no need to defend trash—fold preserves edge for final hand and exploits my deep stack advantage long-term.

This is the final hand of the session. I am on the button with 4h 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. My stack is roughly 39 big blinds. While opening from the button is generally a good strategy to steal blinds, 45o is near the bottom of the range and frequently a fold in this position against active opponents like Elon and Claude. To preserve my stack and maximize my final total, I will choose the conservative path and fold.

Preflop in small blind, folded to me vs big blind Claude. Holding KJo, a strong hand with good equity, playability, and fold equity. Standard play is to raise to isolate and steal the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x BB size) builds the pot while maintaining aggression. Deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility if called. Out of position but KJo hits boards well enough.

This is the final hand (150 of 150), playing heads-up in the big blind with A4o against Elon's small blind raise. 

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: A4o is weak but playable in heads-up situations, especially from the big blind
2. Position: I'm in the big blind, which is favorable post-flop
3. Pot odds: I need to call 200 to win 400 (including my already-posted blind), giving me 2:1 odds - reasonable for marginal hands in heads-up play
4. Stack depth: With 14,000 chips vs Elon's 21,675, I have decent playability post-flop
5. Opponent: Elon has been raising frequently as small blind (standard SB strategy)

A4o merits a call here. I have reasonable equity against his small blind raising range, position advantage post-flop, and the pot odds support continuing. I'll see a flop and play based on the board texture and action.

Flop is Qc 5s 3h, rainbow dry board. My KcJd completely misses, no pair or draw, but as preflop raiser from SB, I have range advantage over Claude's BB defending range. Board favors my range with possible Qx, sets, overpairs. King blocks AK/AQ, jack blocks QJ. Standard spot to c-bet for fold equity and thin value from worse Qx or floats. OOP, but dry texture allows it. Size half-pot (300 into 600) balances value and bluffs, maintains pressure. Deep stacks, but low SPR post-bet keeps playability if called. Checking risks giving free card to draws or letting him realize equity cheaply.

Folding A4o on the 5s-Qc-3h flop in the big blind. This hand is too weak (ace-high with poor kicker) to continue against an aggressive opponent from a position disadvantage. While pot odds are marginally close at 3:1, the equity is insufficient. With deeper stacks remaining and multiple streets ahead, calling here puts me in difficult spots. Better to preserve the 13,800 chip stack and exit the final hand avoiding marginal situations.
